coredata.dc:description: All-solid-state batteries (ASSBs) are emerging as next-generation energy storage solutions due to their potential advantages, including enhanced safety, higher energy density, and broader operational temperature ranges. Among various solid electrolytes, amorphous and crystalline Li4PS4I, have attracted interest due to their predicted high conductivity, and high moisture-tolerance. However, experimental studies have reported a wide variation in conductivity values for Li4PS4I, ranging from 0.03 to 3.5 mS.cm−1 at 298 K which are significantly lower than theoretical predictions. Herein, by employing a combination of X-ray diffraction (XRD), 31P magic-angle spinning nuclear magnetic resonance (31P MAS NMR), electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), we demonstrate that controlling the crystallinity of Li4PS4I plays a crucial role in its electrochemical performance. Pair distribution function (PDF) analysis reveals the differences in local atomic arrangements between amorphous and crystalline Li4PS4I. Additionally, the analysis indicates that mechanical milling alters the local environment of PS4 tetrahedra and iodide anions, which may explain the discrepancy in conductivity. Furthermore, ASSBs incorporating amorphous-ceramic Li4PS4I in the cathode composite exhibit enhanced cycling stability compared to amorphous Li4PS4I. These findings underscore the potential of tuning crystallinity as an effective approach to optimize the ionic transport properties and cycling performance of ASSBs, paving the way for further advancements in solid electrolytes.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of two crystal structure models of Li4PS4I.
Summary of the syntheses of various Li4PS4I samples.

a) XRD patterns of reaction mixture at various milling times. b) Raman spectra of the reaction mixture at various milling times. c) Chronological evolution of the amount of PxSy species during the milling as determined by Raman spectral deconvolution.

Le Bail (profile) fit of the synchrotron and neutron diffraction of the Li4PS4I-250 sample using a) space group P4/mmm with Z = 2 b) space group P4/mmm with Z = 4. Rietveld refinement against neutron diffraction data of the Li4PS4I-250 sample using c) space group P4/nmm with Z = 2, d) space group P4/nmm with Z = 4, a = b = 8.479; c = 11.882 (Å).

a) XRD patterns of Li4PS4I-amorphous, Li4PS4I-ball milled, and Li4PS4I annealed at various temperatures. The amorphous Li4PS4I sample is denoted as Li4PS4I-AM. The ball-milled Li4PS4I sample is denoted as Li4PS4I-BM. The other samples are denoted Li4PS4I-xxx where xxx indicates the annealing temperatures. b) Zoom of figure a from 27 to 30 degrees.
a) Comparison of 31P NMR spectra of various Li4PS4I samples. b) Deconvolution of the NMR spectrum of Li4PS4I-180 sample.

a) Comparison of the ionic conductivity and activation energies of Li4PS4I-AM, Li4PS4I-BM and Li4PS4I annealed at different temperatures. b) Correlation between conductivity and degree of crystallization.

Experimental PDF of Li4PS4I-250 sample and the simulated partial PDFs computed from the refined P4/nmm (Z = 4) structural model of the Li4PS4I-250 sample. b) Fit to a G(r) of the sample Li4PS4I-250 using P4/nmm model from 10 to 30 Å, c) Comparison of the fits to a G(r) of the sample Li4PS4I-250 from 1.5 to 10 Å using average structure model and rotational disorder model.

Comparison of (a) average structure model: SG P4/nmm (Z = 4) (black square) of Li4PS4I-250 and (b) rotational disorder model, which can be described by a space group P4212. Li ions are omitted for clarity. PS4 3− tetrahedra was depicted in gray.

a) Comparison of the experimental pair distribution function of Li4PS4I-AM and Li4PS4I-250 samples. b) Histogram of IS and II interatomic distance. The histogram was constructed based on the average structure model of Li4PS4I-250.

Electrochemical performance results of NMC622-SE/Li4PS4I-AM/Li0.5In-Li4PS4I-AM ASSBs at room temperature. Three electrolytes were tested in the cathode composite, namely, Li4PS4I-AM, Li4PS4I-BM, and Li4PS4I-250. a) Voltage profiles of the first galvanostatic charge−discharge cycle, b) discharge capacity as a function of the cycle number, c) cell polarization over cycling e − f) differential capacity curve of three batteries.

Evolution of Nyquist plot of a) NMC622-Li4PS4I-AM / Li4PS4I-AM / Li0.5In-Li4PS4I-AM battery, b) NMC622-Li4PS4I-BM / Li4PS4I-AM / Li0.5In-Li4PS4I-AM, c) NMC622-Li4PS4I-250 / Li4PS4I-AM / Li0.5In-Li4PS4I-AM during cycling.

Crystallographic data from the Rietveld refinements of synchrotron diffractograms of Li4PS4I-250. SG: P4/nmm. a = b = 8.47915(3), c = 11.88100(7), V/Z = 213.549(2). Reliability parameters: Rp:5.37, Rwp: 5.97, RBragg: 1.77, Chi2: 27.6.
Quantification of degree of crystallization (DOC) of the Li4PS4I samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Due to the absence of Bragg reflections on the diffractogram of the Li4PS4I-AM sample, it is not possible to perform Rietveld refinement, and therefore, determining the degree of crystallization by XRD method.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: All-solid-state batteries (ASSBs) are emerging as next-generation energy storage solutions due to their potential advantages, including enhanced safety, higher energy density, and broader operational temperature ranges. Among various solid electrolytes, amorphous and crystalline Li4PS4I, have attracted interest due to their predicted high conductivity, and high moisture-tolerance. However, experimental studies have reported a wide variation in conductivity values for Li4PS4I, ranging from 0.03 to 3.5 mS.cm−1 at 298 K which are significantly lower than theoretical predictions. Herein, by employing a combination of X-ray diffraction (XRD), 31P magic-angle spinning nuclear magnetic resonance (31P MAS NMR), electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), we demonstrate that controlling the crystallinity of Li4PS4I plays a crucial role in its electrochemical performance. Pair distribution function (PDF) analysis reveals the differences in local atomic arrangements between amorphous and crystalline Li4PS4I. Additionally, the analysis indicates that mechanical milling alters the local environment of PS4 tetrahedra and iodide anions, which may explain the discrepancy in conductivity. Furthermore, ASSBs incorporating amorphous-ceramic Li4PS4I in the cathode composite exhibit enhanced cycling stability compared to amorphous Li4PS4I. These findings underscore the potential of tuning crystallinity as an effective approach to optimize the ionic transport properties and cycling performance of ASSBs, paving the way for further advancements in solid electrolytes.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium thiophosphates represent a promising class of solid electrolytes, encompassing materials such as LGPS-type Li10GeP2S12, [1–3] argyrodite-type Li6PS5X (X = Br, Cl), [4–6] and thio-LISICON or Li2S-P2S5-based compounds [7,8]. These materials have demonstrated relatively high conductivity (10−1–10 mS.cm−1) and favorable mechanical properties, making them strong candidates for solid-state battery applications [9]. Among sulfide electrolytes, those in the Li2S − P2S5 binary system may not offer the highest conductivity but remain a significant area of research due to their intriguing structural properties and (electro-)chemical stability. Additionally, these solid electrolytes do not require (semi-)metallic elements such as Si, Ge, Al, which could potentially lower production costs. Although the thiophosphate electrolytes in the Li2S-P2S5 binary system show interesting features, none of them satisfies all the requirements for a practical solid electrolyte. For example, Li3PS4 and Li7P3S11 have a conductivity higher than 10−1 mS.cm−1 at RT but are moisture sensitive [10,11]. On the other hand, Li4P2S6, and Li2P2S6 are more stable but poorly conductive [12]. These limitations have driven research toward modifying these electrolytes and one common approach to enhancing conductivity and stability is the incorporation of lithium halides. Along this line, several halide-containing electrolytes have been investigated, notably, Li4PS4I, 2Li2S-P2S5-xLiI, 7Li2S-3P2S5-xLiI [13,14]. In general, improvement in conductivity was observed by up to factor of 5 upon adding LiI as observed in the case of 2Li2S-P2S5-xLiI [13]. Using computational methods and molecular dynamics simulations (MD), several lithium thiophosphate halides along the Li3PS4-LiX tie line, including Li4PS4I, Li5PS4Cl2, and a newly predicted compound, Li15(PS4)4Cl3 was studied [15]. Among these, Li4PS4I is of particular interest, as it is predicted to feature a disorder Li sublattice, potentially reaching conductivity of 10−1 mS.cm−1. Another theoretical study based on DFT and MD predicted that amorphous and crystalline Li4PS4I could have conductivities exceeding 100 mS.cm−1 [16]. Beside ionic transport, incorporating LiI has also been reported to improve electrochemical and chemical stabilities as demonstrated in amorphous-ceramic 3Li2S-P2S5-1LiI (Li7P2S8I) and crystalline Li4PS4I [17–21].
The crystal structure of Li4PS4I, however, remains a subject of debate, with two competing structural models proposed in the literature.

The first one, developed by Sedlmaier et al. (Model 1, Fig. 1 ), describes Li4PS4I, synthesized via solvent-assisted methods, using the tetragonal P4/nmm space group with a = b = 8.484 Å, c = 5.937 Å, V/Z = 213.67 Å3/f.u [22]. Neutron powder diffraction data revealed a disordered Li substructure, where Li atoms partially occupy five Wyckoff positions, with the 8i site (Li(5)) having the lowest occupancy (0.08). A follow-up study by Strauss et al. on mechanochemically synthesized Li4PS4I used a similar structural model, suggesting that Li(5) remains weakly occupied at room temperature and becomes unobservable at 10 K [23]. Recently, Nazar et al. challenged this description, proposing a superstructure variant of Li4PS4I with a doubled c-axis (c = 11.834 Å, V/Z = 211.99 Å3/f.u) (Model 2) [24]. While the anion framework remains similar, Model 2 lacks the fifth Li site and describes Li as fully occupying four sites, in contrast to the partial disorder reported in Model 1. This structural distinction has profound implications for ionic transport: Model 1 suggests a 3D conduction pathway, whereas Model 2 predicts a 2D conduction mechanism in (a,b) - type planes, as inferred from DFT, MD, and BVS simulations [16,24]. The discrepancy between these models may stem from different synthesis methods. Nazar et al. employed a solid-state synthesis, which involved annealing at 700 °C, resulting in Li4PS4I phase without crystalline impurities. In contrast, the Li4PS4I obtained in Sedlmaier's and Strauss's work contains roughly 10 wt% of crystalline impurities (Li2S, LiI, Li6PS5I), potentially lead to off-stoichiometric materials. Additionally, mechanochemical synthesis or liquid-assisted synthesis can stabilize metastable structures, as well-known for β-Li3PS4 and β-Na3PS4 [7,25–28]. This might explain why Sedlmaier et al. and Strauss's et al. observed a disordered Li4PS4I structure. Motivated by these inconsistencies, we systematically investigate whether mechanochemical synthesis affects the resulting Li sublattice, aiming to provide a conclusive structural model for Li4PS4I.

In addition to the ambiguous crystal structure of Li4PS4I, several studies noticed that the annealing/heat treatment temperature highly impacts the ionic conductivity of Li4PS4I. Strauss et al. observed that X-ray amorphous Li4PS4I (without annealing) exhibited a conductivity of

∼ 1.3 mS.cm−1 at 298 K [29]. The same trend was also observed for Li4PS4I synthesized via solvent-assisted or hot-pressing method [30]. Despite these observations, the structural differences between amorphous and crystalline Li4PS4I remain unexplored. Recently, Wilkening et al. investigated nanocrystalline and microcrystalline Li4PS4I using EIS and NMR techniques [31]. Their 31P NMR data analysis suggested that 35–50 % of phosphorus atoms in nanocrystalline Li4PS4I experience distortions or point defects. The finding is corroborated by 6Li NMR data, which revealed an additional population of mobile Li ions residing near disordered interfacial regions. These structural irregularities significantly altered the attempt frequencies observed in EIS measurements and thus was proposed to enhance the ionic conductivity of nanocrystalline Li4PS4I.

In this study, we demonstrate that a controlled combination of mechanical milling and heat treatments can effectively tune the crystallinity of Li4PS4I solid electrolyte and subsequently influence its physico-chemical properties. First, we show, by using X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectroscopy, that the milling duration could alter the local structure of an amorphous-ceramic Li4PS4I. We then report on the possible rotational displacement of PS4 tetrahedra in crystalline Li4PS4I and provide the first structural insight of amorphous Li4PS4I by using pair distribution function analysis. Using 31P magic-angle-spinning nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (31P MAS NMR), and XRD, we quantitatively evaluate the crystallinity in our Li4PS4I samples and correlate it with ionic transport property. Finally, we report promising results indicating that all-solid-state batteries employing amorphous-ceramic Li4PS4I in the cathode composite exhibit superior cycling stability compared to those utilizing amorphous Li4PS4I.

Mechano-chemical syntheses were employed to prepare Li4PS4I with tuneable crystallinity. Specifically, a 4 g mixture of stoichiometric amount of Li2S (Sigma-Aldrich, 99.98 %), P2S5 (Sigma-Aldrich, 99 %), LiI (Thermo Scientific Chemicals, 99 %) precursors were weighed and placed in a 45-mL zirconia milling pot with fifteen 10-mm zirconia balls. The milling speed was set at 700 rpm. The milling program consists of 152 cycles, each cycle being composed of 15 min of milling followed by 15 min of pause to prevent overheating. After milling, the powder was recovered from the milling pot and part of it was pelletized under 254 MPa and subjected to annealing in an evacuated quartz tube for 1 h. Another part of the powder was subjected to an amorphization step through a soft ball-milling conditions. The milling was operated at 510 rpm, for 96 cycles (15 min of milling, 15 min of pause) using 50 g of 3-mm zirconia balls.

Powder X-ray diffraction was performed using a Bruker D8 Advance powder diffractometer, with a copper anode (Cu-Kα = 1.5418 Å). The sample were prepared in LeRiChe'S cell) [32], which were capped with a Be window to avoid air exposure. The powder XRD pattern was fitted using the Rietveld method as implemented in the FullProf Suite program, with a Thompson-Cox-Hastings pseudo-Voigt function to model the peaks shape [33]. The background was described by linear interpolation between a set of manual points with adjustable heights. Scale factor, zero shift, background points, lattice parameters, shape profile parameters, asymmetric parameters and atomic coordinates were refined. Attempts to refine atomic displacement parameters resulted in unphysical values and thus was kept fixed during the refinement.

Neutron diffraction experiments were conducted at the Institut Laue-Langevin, ILL, in Grenoble, France. The powder diffraction data were acquired in constant wavelength mode with a wavelength of 1.594 Å using the D2B diffractometer. The measurements were performed on ∼1.2 g powder, contained in 6.5 mm diameter vanadium sample cans at 298 K. Experiments were performed in the range 0.1° ≤ 2θ ≤ 159.95°, with a step width of 0.05°.

The Synchrotron XRD experiments were performed at the MSPD beamline at the ALBA synchrotron beamline, in Barcelona, Spain. The powder samples were sealed in a quartz capillary and the data were collected in transmission mode using ten position-sensitive detector MYTHEN2 with a wavelength of 0.8269 Å in the 2Theta range from 0.5° ≤ 2θ ≤ 59.198° in 0.006° steps.

31P Magic Angle Spinning (MAS) NMR experiments were conducted using a Bruker Advance Neo Spectrometer, operating at a magnetic field strength of 9.4 T, corresponding to Larmor frequencies of 162.0 MHz for 31P. The NMR sample was packed into a 4.0 mm zirconia rotor and spun at 14 kHz. The 31P MAS NMR spectra were acquired using single-pulse excitation. With a 4.7 μs π/2 pulse length and a 500 s recycle delay. The 31P spectra were referenced to H3PO4 85 % (□ = 0 ppm). Data processing was performed in Topspin (Bruker), and spectral deconvolution was carried out using DMFit software [34].

A small amount of sample was placed on a glass slide and sealed using Scotch tape to minimize air exposure. Raman spectra were recorded using a DXR Raman Microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific) equipped with a 532 nm laser operating at a power of 5 mW. Spectral analysis and fitting were carried out using Omnic Software (Thermo Fisher Scientific).

Powders were sealed in 0.3 mm capillaries and measured in Debye-Scherrer geometry using a Bruker D8 diffractometer with Mo Kα source (λ = 0.71 Å) and a DECTRIS EIGER2 500 K detector. Total scattering data up to a Qmax ∼17 Å−1 were obtained by an optimized data collection strategy. Air scattering and capillary absorption were corrected by measuring the total scattering of an empty instrument and an empty capillary. Data reduction of reciprocal space data to real space G (r) data was performed in the DIFFRAC.EVA V7 (Bruker) software. Instrument broadening parameters (Qbroad and Qdamp) were extracted from a measurement performed on a capillary of NIST SRM 640d Si powder. Real-space fitting, and calculation of the simulated partial correlations, was performed with the PDFgui software package [35].

Electrochemical Impedance spectroscopy was performed on Li4PS4I pellets. Measurements were conducted using a BioLogic MTZ-35 impedance apparatus. Approximately 200 mg of powder were cold pressed at 636 MPa into a pellet. The pellet was then placed between two gold electrodes and kept inside an airtight sample holder. Data was collected in a frequency range of 3.5 × 107 Hz to 0.1 Hz, with applied potential of 50 mV over a temperature range of −10 °C to 25 °C under an Ar atmosphere.

All-solid-state batteries (ASSBs) with the configuration In/InLi-SE | SE | NMC622-SE ASSB cells were assembled as follows. First, 75 mg of amorphous Li4PS4I powder was compressed under 1 ton of pressure in a PMMA mold (10 mm in diameter) to form a mechanically robust separator. The NMC622-SE composite cathodes were prepared via hand-mixing the NMC622 (Targray), and SE in a 70:30 weight ratio. The LiIn0.5-SE powder was prepared by manually grinding Li and In metallic foils (Sigma-Aldrich) with amorphous Li4PS4I. To assemble the cell, approximately 24 mg of the composite cathode was uniformly spread onto one side of the separator, while 85 mg of LiIn0.5-Li4PS4I-AM powder was evenly distributed onto the other side of the separator. The entire cell was then pressed at 2 tons for 30 s to ensure proper contact. The cell was placed in a custom-made airtight pot and subjected to galvanostatic cyclings using a Biologic BCS-800 battery cycler. The cells were cycled between 1.9 and 3.6 V vs. In/InLi, at 25 °C under a stack pressure of approximately 100 MPa. The in-situ EIS measurements were performed on the same apparatus over a frequency range from 10 kHz to 1 Hz and applied potential of 10 mV at the end of each charge and discharge cycle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Li4PS4I with varying crystallinity
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, to prepare partial amorphous-crystalline Li4PS4I samples, we employed mechano-milling follow by annealing at various temperatures (180, 220, 250 and 350 °C) for one hour. A complete X-ray amorphous sample was prepared by a second milling step, as depicted in Fig. 2 (see details in the Materials and Methods section). Mechanical milling can create local pressure in the order of magnitude of gigapascals (GPa) [34], which can induce sliding of lattice planes and/or dislocations, and therefore, resulting in loss of long-range order. Pressure-induced amorphization has been reported for wide variety of materials such as ice-water, SiO2, GeO2, semiconductor alloys, metallic glasses, biomolecules, etc. [36]. Schneider et al. recently reported that compacting the Li7SiPS8 sulfide electrolyte at a pressure of 1.7 GPa can indeed slightly increase the amorphous fraction [37]. Besides complete loss of long-range order, mechanical grinding can also reduce crystallinity as observed in various materials, including sulfide ionic conductors, such as Li6PS5I and Li10P2GeS12 [38–44]. Remarkably, after mechanical treatment, conductivities were reported to increase in some cases such as for Li6PS5I, Ag3PS4, LiTaO3, LiAlSi2O6 [31,45,46]. The underlying reasons are attributed to particle size reduction, reduced crystallite size, accumulated stress or defects such as vacancies, stacking faults or dislocations. On the contrary, several materials, notably Li10P2GeS12 or LiAlSi4O10 show lower conductivity after being partially amorphized [45]. This motivated us to examine how the amorphization influenced the conductivity of Li4PS4I. Notably, in the course of our study, the partial amorphization of Li4PS4I by mechanical milling was reported by the Wilkening group [31]. Their obtained Li4PS4I displays Bragg reflections and is termed nanocrystalline Li4PS4I.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the milling duration on the local structure
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies on Li4PS4I have mainly focused on the effect of synthesis conditions on ionic conductivities rather than on structural changes [29,30]. Therefore, we first explored the effect of the milling time on the local structure of Li4PS4I. The XRD patterns of the Li4PS4I samples obtained after different milling times are shown in Fig. 3a. After 6 h of milling, the sample already contains crystalline Li4PS4I at ∼20.8o and 28.0o together with poorly crystalline LiI and Li2S precursors. Continuing milling for longer periods further completes the reaction and promotes the growth of the crystalline domains of Li4PS4I as the peaks of the main phase become sharper. Raman spectroscopy was used as a complementary technique to XRD to monitor the local structure evolution during the synthesis. After 6 h of milling, the sample exhibits a main peak around 420 cm−1, a shoulder around 405 cm−1 and a small signal around 386 cm−1 (Fig. 3b). To the best of our knowledge, there has been no clear assignment of the Raman bands for Li4PS4I material before. However, based on the reported Raman spectroscopies on thiophosphate glasses □-Li3PS4, we assigned the peaks around 420, 405 and 386 cm−1 to PS4 3−, P2S7 4− and P2S6 4−, respectively [25,47,48]. As no crystalline Li4P2S6 was observed by XRD, we speculate that the P2S6 4− and P2S7 4− units exist as local defects in the amorphous Li4PS4I phase.

The relative quantities of PxSy species can be determined by deconvoluting the Raman spectra. All the Raman spectra consistently show that the bands at around 420 cm−1 are composed of a broad contribution at 419–422 cm−1 and a narrower one at 423–426 cm−1 (Fig. S1). As the local environment in an amorphous material is expected to be subject to greater variations, the wider peak at 419–422 cm−1 is tentatively assigned to the PS4 3− unit in the amorphous phase while the other one is attributed to PS4 3− in crystalline Li4PS4I. The signals related to the P2S7 4− and P2S6 4− moieties are greatly reduced in the samples obtained after 18 h of milling and reduced to only 1.3 % of the total PxSy species after 37 h of milling (Fig. 3c). To further eliminate the moieties, however, it requires extending the reaction time to 76 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Re-examination of the crystal structure of crystalline Li4PS4I
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned earlier, the description of the crystal structure of Li4PS4I is still under debate. It remains an open question whether the mechanochemical synthesis can stabilize the disorder structure (Model 1) or this synthesis method also leads to the order structure (Model 2). To provide a conclusive answer in this regard, we performed laboratory powder X-ray diffraction, synchrotron X-ray diffraction (MSPD beamline, ALBA, Barcelona) and neutron diffraction (D2B, ILL, Grenoble) on the samples that were annealed at 250 °C, 180 °C, 320 °C. The samples were denoted hereafter Li4PS4I-250, Li4PS4I-180 and Li4PS4I-320.

The synchrotron diffractogram of the Li4PS4I-250 sample was initially indexed using the space group P4/mmm which corresponds to the Laue group of the primitive tetragonal cell with a = b = 8.479 Å, c = 5.940 Å, V/Z = 213.52 Å3/fu. However, a closer inspection of the Lebail fit clearly indicates that the unit cell cannot explain all the observed reflections (marked asterisk) as shown in Fig. 4a. Taking into account the reflections, a new tetragonal cell was found, which is described by the space group P4/nmm with a = b = 8.479, c = 11.882 Å, V/Z = 213.56 Å3/f.u, similar to Model 2 (Fig. 4b). Atomic positions were then determined using a direct-space method implemented in FOX, which simultaneously incorporated both X-ray and neutron data. [49] The obtained atomic positions closely resembled those of Model 2. To refine these positions with higher accuracy, subsequent Rietveld refinements were performed using the FullProf Suite [33]. Model 2 demonstrated an excellent fit of both synchrotron (Figure S2) and neutron data. Additionally, by comparing the Rietveld refinement against the neutron data using Model 1 and Model 2, we also notice that Model 1 fails to explain the reflections around 23.4, 25.5, 33.9 and 39.2o (Error! Reference source not found.c). Model 2, on the other hand, significantly improves the fit (Fig. 4d). Based on our analysis, we confirm that Model 2, proposed by the Nazar group, [24] correctly describes the observed diffraction data of crystalline Li4PS4I. The superstructure reflections were also observed in the Li4PS4I-180 and Li4PS4I-320 samples, which are annealed at other temperatures (180 °C and 320 °C, see Fig. S3 and S4). Therefore, we unambiguously demonstrate that the mechanochemical synthesis yields an ordered structure regardless of annealing temperatures. The full crystallographic characteristics are listed in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of annealing temperature on degree of crystallization
ce:section.ce:para: Having confirmed the structural model for crystalline Li4PS4I, it was then possible to determine the impact of annealing on the crystal structure and degree of crystallization. The XRD patterns of the samples are shown in Fig. 5 .

Starting with the Li4PS4I-BM sample, we can clearly observe broad Bragg reflections corresponding to the Li4PS4I phase. The amorphization of the sample yields a Li4PS4I-AM sample which does not exhibit any Bragg reflections when measured with the laboratory Cu diffractometer. We consider this sample as completely amorphous. Annealing the sample led to the growth of crystalline domains as evidenced by the narrowing of the Bragg reflections. Additionally, the diffraction peaks are shifted to higher two-theta angles, suggesting the contraction of the unit cell. To quantify the degree of crystallization, Rietveld refinements were performed using an internal standard method, with highly crystalline Si (Sigma Aldrich, 99.9 %). The amount of crystalline Li4PS4I could be calculated based on the weight of Si standard. The degree of crystallization is then determined based on the weight ratio of crystalline Li4PS4I over the total mass of Li4PS4I sample. The results are presented in Table 2 . Rietveld refinement patterns and detailed results are presented in Fig. S5.

The amount of amorphous Li4PS4I in various samples was further investigated, and confirmed, by 31P NMR. Fig. 6a shows an interesting difference between the Li4PS4I samples. The Li4PS4I-BM sample (black curve in Fig. 6a) displays a pronounced asymmetric feature, with a main peak at around 81.9 ppm and a broad shoulder in the range of 84–86 ppm, as often observable in Li2S-P2S5 glasses and glass-ceramics [50]. After amorphization, the phosphorous local environment appears to be more homogeneous as the Li4PS4I-AM sample (blue curve in Fig. 6a) displays a broad, seemingly symmetric feature, centering around 83.86 ppm, indicating a wide, random distribution of the local environment surrounding the 31P nuclei. Upon annealing the Li4PS4I-BM sample, a peak at around 81.9 ppm sharpens and the wide shoulder gradually diminishes as the annealing temperature increases. This prompted us to assign the sharp peak at 81.9 ppm to the PS4 3− unit in crystalline Li4PS4I domains, in excellent agreement with previously reported values [22,31]. Owing to the different line shapes, we were able to deconvolute the spectra and quantify the amount of PS4 3− in the amorphous matrix (noted a-PS4 3−) and PS4 3− in the crystalline domains (noted c-PS4 3−) as illustrated in Fig. 6b. Table 2 shows the degree of crystallization determined using 31P NMR compared to the values obtained from XRD. Both methods reveal that the degree of crystallization increases sharply by annealing at temperatures from 180 °C. Annealing at 320 °C completely transforms amorphous Li4PS4I into crystalline Li4PS4I.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of degree of crystallization on conductivity
ce:section.ce:para: The conductivities of the sample series were investigated using EIS, as presented in Fig. 7a. Notably, thermal annealing resulted in a decrease in conductivity by more than one order of magnitude, accompanied by a substantial increase in the activation energy from 0.40 eV in Li4PS4I-AM to 0.55 eV in Li4PS4I-320 samples. This correlation suggests a structural transition impacting ionic transport as shown in Fig. 7b, clearly demonstrate that the degree of crystallization strongly influences the ionic conductivity. An increasing proportion of the amorphous Li4PS4I structure correlates positively with an enhancement in ionic conductivity while simultaneously leading to a reduction in the activation energy. However, it should be noted that, as indicated by the NMR spectra, the line width of the c-PS4 3− peak varies across the samples, suggesting that the crystalline domains in the annealed samples are different, which may also impact the conductivity to a certain degree. An heterogeneous nature of Li diffusion in amorphous-ceramic Li4PS4I was suggested by Wilkening et al., as evidenced by a two-component line shape observed by 7Li NMR [31]. Nevertheless, the robust correlation illustrated in Fig. 7b strongly implies that variations in the conductivity of our series of samples can be fundamentally attributed to the variation of degrees of crystallization. Our result further corroborates the study reported by Wilkening et al., which showed that nano-Li4PS4I material composes of highly distorted interfacial regions, which might lead to the enhancement of the macroscopic conductivity compared to the micro-Li4PS4I material [31]. However, it is worth mentioning that the origin of the high conductivity of the amorphous Li4PS4I sample has not been investigated. Further studies that combine structural analysis and simulation might shed light on the subject.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Local structures of Li4PS4I-250 and Li4PS4I-AM
ce:section.ce:para: In an attempt to gain a better understanding of the local structural arrangements in both amorphous and crystalline Li4PS4I, total X-ray scattering experiments were performed. The X-ray atomic pair distribution [G(r)] obtained for the crystalline Li4PS4I-250 sample is depicted in Fig. 8a alongside the simulated partial pair distributions calculated from the crystal structure of Li4PS4I (P4/nmm, a = b = 8.479, c = 11.882 Å).

Comparing the relative intensities of the simulated and experimental data it can be seen that the G(r) is dominated by the IS pair distances, for example, at 3.64 and 4.66 Å, which is due to the larger scattering factor of iodine and the greater number of S atoms in the structure. Characteristic peaks at 2.02 and 3.32 Å correspond well to the PS bond and the SS correlation in the PS4 3− unit, respectively. Two weak correlations around 2.6 Å can be assigned to LiS bond distances as previously observed for Li3PS4 [51,52]. The complex features from 6 to 7 Å are attributed to the convolution of IS, II, and SS correlations. A structural model of crystalline Li4PS4I was then refined against the G(r) data. The long-range order (from 10 to 30 Å) can be well described by the average structure as evidenced by the residual value Rw of 10 % (Fig. 8b). However, at shorter ranges, from 1.5 Å to 10 Å, clear local structure deviations from the average model can be seen and the residual value Rw increases to 22.4 % (Fig. 8c). The visual inspection of the fit revealed that the mismatch occurs primarily around 4.6, 5.9, and 7 Å, which is close to the IS, PI, and IS pair distances. Therefore, it was speculated that these deviations from the average model may be due to displacements or tilting of PS4 3− tetrahedra, which is often observed in perovskite materials and recently reported for thiophosphate-containing solid electrolytes. [53–56].

Inspired by the tilt/displacement of the PS4 3− unit observed in the literature and based on the site symmetry of P (2 mm) and S (.m.) in the space group P4/nmm, we propose that the tilt/displacement can be realized if the mirror symmetry element of the P site is removed. One possible symmetry reduction leads to the space group P4212, which improved the fit in the 1.5 to 10 Å range compared to the P4/nmm average structure, with the residual value Rw decreasing from 22.4 % to 14.2 % (Fig. 8c). The corresponding structural comparison between the two models is shown in Fig. 9 . However, these data were collected on a laboratory instrument with limited r-space resolution. Therefore, these results are not fully conclusive. Higher-quality data, in particular total neutron scattering, would provide better insight into the local structural environment and help validate this hypothesis.

The pair distribution function G(r) of the amorphous Li4PS4I-AM and the crystalline Li4PS4I-250 samples are compared in Fig. 10a. Beyond 12 A, the G(r) data of Li4PS4I-AM shows almost no correlations features, indicating the absence of long-range order which is consistent with the XRD results. Both samples show the first sharp peak correlation at 2.02 Å, corresponding to PS bonds within PS4 3− tetrahedra. This suggests that the PS4 3− building blocks remain largely intact after the amorphization process. Apart from this feature, the short-range ordering of the amorphous Li4PS4I-AM is very different from that of the crystalline Li4PS4I-250. This is in contrast with previous studies that reported the similarities in the short range order of amorphous 3Li2S-P2S5 and crystalline □-Li3PS4 or amorphous and annealed 70Li3PS4 – 30LiI [57,58]. To help assign the G(r) of the Li4PS4I-AM sample we plotted a histogram of the frequency of expected IS and II pair distances in ideal crystalline Li4PS4I, as these two pairs largely dominate the X-ray PDF data. For the first coordination shell of I, five pairs distances at 3.66, 3.79, 4.12, 4.2 and 4.62 Å can be expected (Fig. 10b). Considering the number of pairs, we obtained a mean distance value of 4.26 Å, which coincides with the observed distance on the G(r) of amorphous sample. Similarly, a mean value of 6.7 Å is expected for the second IS coordination shell and can be observed as a broad peak at 6.7 Å on the G(r). The complex appearance of the peak is likely due to the overlap of the IS correlation with nearby II correlation. We again note that the PS4 3− tetrahedra are similar in both samples, so it would be reasonable to hypothesize that the PS bonds do not undergo significant modification.

These observations suggest that there may be a displacement and/or tilt of PS4 3− tetrahedra in such a way that the mean distance of the first IS coordination shell is approximately 4.26 Å. The isotropic atomic arrangement observed for PS4 group and I anion were further supported by the symmetric feature of the corresponding 31P NMR data (Fig. 6a). In addition, the displacement of PS4 3− leads to the loss of symmetry and the reduction of coherence domain to a few tens of Å as suggested by the absence of long-range ordering above 15 Å. However, a complete structural description including Li spatial arrangement would require reverse Monte Carlo simulations based on high quality neutron and/or synchrotron total scattering data, which is currently beyond the scope of this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of crystallinity on battery performances
ce:section.ce:para: Taking advantage of our work on the synthesis and quantification of degree of crystallization, we selected 3 electrolytes, namely Li4PS4I-AM, Li4PS4I-BM and Li4PS4I-250 to fabricate ASSBs and study the impact of crystallinity on ASSB performance. All ASSBs were fabricated using Li4PS4I-AM as a separator. The cathode composite was prepared by grinding 70 wt% of NMC622 and 30 wt% of solid electrolyte (Li4PS4I-AM or Li4PS4I-BM or Li4PS4I-250) in a mortar. Detailed cell fabrication and cycling are presented in Materials and Methods section.

The voltage profiles of the first galvanostatic charge/discharge electrochemical cycle and the evolution of the discharge capacity are comparatively shown in Fig. 11
a) and b), respectively. The cell using Li4PS4I-AM as the catholyte material in the composite positive electrode exhibited the highest initial discharge and charge capacities, reaching up to approximately 164 mAh.g−1 at C/30. In comparison, Li4PS4I-BM showed slightly lower capacities and Li4PS4I-250 had the lowest, close to 145 mAh.g−1 (Fig. 11a). This is in line with the relative ionic conductivity of the electrolytes, as their conductivities are 3.4, 2.1 and 0.2 mS.cm−1 for Li4PS4I-AM, Li4PS4I-BM, and Li4PS4I-250, respectively, suggesting that the Li4PS4-AM-based cell benefits from the high conductivity of Li4PS4I-AM catholyte, which supports better de-lithiation of NMC622. In addition to the charge capacity, the voltage profiles are quite different, as evidenced by a smoother profile and reduced polarization in the Li4PS4I-AM based battery.

The rate capability and cycling stability of the batteries were evaluated across C-rates from C/15 to C/2 (Fig. 11b). As the C-rate increased, all samples exhibited a gradual capacity decline, with Li4PS4I-AM maintaining the highest capacity at moderate C-rates, indicating better rate capability. At C/5 and C/2, a sharp capacity drop was observed, though Li4PS4I-AM and Li4PS4I-BM retained around 60 mAh·g−1, while Li4PS4I-250 declined further. At C/2, none of the samples delivered significant capacity. When the C-rate was lowered back to C/15, Li4PS4I-BM and Li4PS4I-250 showed near full capacity recovery, indicating structural integrity, whereas Li4PS4I-AM exhibited limited recovery, suggesting irreversible degradation. This trend was reflected in cell polarization (Fig. 11c). Long cycling at C/15 showed a slight polarization increase in Li4PS4I-AM (0.12 V to 0.15 V), while Li4PS4I-BM remained stable at 0.11 V, suggesting that Li4PS4I-BM is more thermodynamically stable than Li4PS4I-AM. Differential capacity analysis further highlights key differences in electrochemical stability. In Li4PS4I-AM, the peak at 3.17 V (second cycle) shifted to 3.24 V (45th cycle) with reduced intensity, indicating increased overpotential and capacity fading. In contrast, Li4PS4I-BM remained stable, with only a slight shift from 3.20 V to 3.21 V, suggesting better retention. Li4PS4I -250 exhibited the most significant peak shift (3.25 V to 3.32 V) and capacity degradation, confirming its inferior electrochemical stability (Fig. 11d-f).

To investigate the capacity fading mechanisms, we analyzed the evolution of EIS spectra over cycling (Fig. 12 ). Although EIS data was recorded within a limited frequency range (10 kHz to 1 Hz) due to instrument constraints, it still provides insights into electrolyte/electrode interfacial resistance. The EIS trends reveal distinct stability differences among the three batteries. Li4PS4I-BM exhibits the most stable impedance growth, indicating a stable electrolyte/electrode interface suitable for long-term cycling. In contrast, Li4PS4I-AM shows a significant impedance increase, suggesting rapid interfacial degradation. Although the exact resistance contributions from the cathode/electrolyte and Li-In/electrolyte interfaces could not be separately quantified, we estimate that total interfacial resistance in Li4PS4I-AM increases by ∼200 % from the 1st to the 45th charge cycle, highlighting its accelerated impedance buildup. Meanwhile, the battery using crystalline Li4PS4I-250, exhibits the highest initial impedance but maintains a moderate increase over cycling, offering better stability than Li4PS4I-AM but inferior to Li4PS4I-BM. This comparison underscores the role of electrolyte crystallinity in interfacial stability, with Li4PS4I-BM demonstrating the most controlled impedance growth, making it the strongest candidate for long-term cycling, whereas Li4PS4I-AM appears more prone to capacity degradation. Wang et al. reported that crystalline β-Li3PS4 is more prone to contact loss compared to glassy Li2S-P2S5. Additionally, more insulating polysulfides form in β-Li3PS4-C65 cathodes [59]. In our case, amorphous-crystalline Li4PS4I-BM exhibits better stability than amorphous Li4PS4I-AM. Future XPS measurements are expected to shed light into the chemical reaction between Li4PS4I and NMC622.

In this study, we have demonstrated that controlling the degree of crystallization of Li4PS4I through a combination of mechanical milling and heat treatment significantly influences its structural and electrochemical properties. Our investigation, using XRD, Raman spectroscopy, and PDF, revealed that mechanical milling induces local structural modifications in PS4 tetrahedra and iodide anions, potentially explaining the wide variation in Li4PS4I conductivity reported in the literature. Furthermore, XRD, 31P MAS NMR and EIS provided quantitative insights into crystallinity and its correlation with ionic transport. The impact of crystallinity on battery performance was further evaluated in all-solid-state batteries (ASSBs), where amorphous-ceramic Li4PS4I in the cathode composite exhibited superior cycling stability compared to its fully amorphous counterpart. These findings underscore the importance of crystallinity tuning as a viable strategy to optimize ionic conductivity and battery performance. By providing a deeper understanding of the structure-properties relationship in Li4PS4I, this work contributes to the ongoing development of thiophosphate-based solid electrolytes and ASSB technology.
